prompt: Which choice best describes the relationship between the two texts?

choices.text: both texts conclude that self-driving cars should be banned outright
choices.rationale: Neither text calls for an outright ban.

choices.text: both texts agree that the technology is already fully trustworthy
choices.rationale: Text 2 says trust must be earned slowly, not that it is already full.

choices.text: Text 1 emphasizes a likely benefit while Text 2 emphasizes unresolved concerns
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 highlights safety benefits; Text 2 highlights open concerns.

choices.text: Text 2 supplies detailed statistics that confirm the claim in Text 1
choices.rationale: Text 2 raises concerns rather than confirming Text 1 with statistics.

explanation: Text 1 stresses a benefit; Text 2 stresses unresolved concerns.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Self-driving cars, the author predicts, will sharply cut crashes, since most accidents trace to human error such as distraction or fatigue. Machines, the text says, do not text, tire, or drink.
stimulus.text_b: Handing driving to software, this author warns, raises hard questions software cannot easily answer, from split-second moral choices to who is at fault in a crash. Trust, the text argues, must be earned slowly.

prompt: Based on the texts, the author of Text 2 would most likely respond to Text 1's optimism by

choices.text: raising unresolved questions of responsibility and judgment
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 stresses unsettled moral and liability questions.

choices.text: arguing that human drivers never grow distracted or fatigued
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not claim humans are never distracted.

choices.text: denying that human error causes any traffic crashes whatsoever
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not deny human error causes crashes.

choices.text: agreeing that the technology has no remaining drawbacks at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 raises drawbacks rather than dismissing them.

explanation: Text 2 would counter the optimism with unresolved questions of judgment and fault.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Self-driving cars, the author predicts, will sharply cut crashes, since most accidents trace to human error such as distraction or fatigue. Machines, the text says, do not text, tire, or drink.
stimulus.text_b: Handing driving to software, this author warns, raises hard questions software cannot easily answer, from split-second moral choices to who is at fault in a crash. Trust, the text argues, must be earned slowly.

prompt: The author of Text 2 would most likely respond to Text 1's emphasis on convenience by

choices.text: acknowledging the convenience but giving priority to comprehension
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 values comprehension over the conveniences Text 1 cites.

choices.text: denying outright that screens are able to store many texts at once
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not dispute storage capacity.

choices.text: agreeing that printed books should be abandoned in the classroom
choices.rationale: Text 2 wants to keep print central.

choices.text: claiming that the font size of screen text can never be adjusted
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not make that claim.

explanation: Text 2 would grant the convenience yet stress comprehension.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reading on screens lets students carry an entire library, search text instantly, and adjust font size for comfort. For many learners, the author notes, these tools make reading more accessible, not less.
stimulus.text_b: Studies suggest readers often comprehend and remember more from print than from screens, perhaps because paper invites slower, more focused attention. The author urges keeping print central in classrooms.

prompt: Based on the texts, the two authors would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: a typical library building actually contains physical books
choices.rationale: Both accept libraries hold books.

choices.text: the font size of text can be adjusted on an electronic screen
choices.rationale: Both would accept that screens adjust font size.

choices.text: students should be permitted to read texts of any kind at all
choices.rationale: Both assume students read.

choices.text: reading on a screen is as good as print for the purpose of learning
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 praises screens; Text 2 favors print for comprehension.

explanation: The dispute is whether screens match print for learning.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reading on screens lets students carry an entire library, search text instantly, and adjust font size for comfort. For many learners, the author notes, these tools make reading more accessible, not less.
stimulus.text_b: Studies suggest readers often comprehend and remember more from print than from screens, perhaps because paper invites slower, more focused attention. The author urges keeping print central in classrooms.

prompt: How would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to Text 1's claim that light is 'a basic service a city owes its residents'?

choices.text: by denying that residents have any need for streetlights at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 suggests lighting what needs it, not no light at all.

choices.text: by agreeing some light is owed but arguing against flooding the night with it
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 would accept lighting what truly needs it while opposing excess.

choices.text: by claiming that artificial light cannot affect the sleep of animals
choices.rationale: Text 2 explicitly cites disrupted animal movement and sleep.

choices.text: by insisting that cities should make every street far brighter still
choices.rationale: Text 2 opposes, rather than urges, ever-brighter streets.

explanation: Text 2 would grant a need for some light yet reject lighting in excess.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Brighter streetlights, the author argues, help people feel safe, let drivers see clearly, and keep public spaces usable after dark. Light, the text says, is a basic service a city owes its residents.
stimulus.text_b: Flooding the night with light, this author warns, wastes energy, disrupts the sleep of people and the movement of nocturnal animals, and erases the stars. Cities, the text suggests, could light only what truly needs it.

prompt: Based on the texts, the authors would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: residents of a city ever travel outdoors after sunset
choices.rationale: Both accept residents go out after dark.

choices.text: cities should provide abundant artificial light at night
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 favors abundant light; Text 2 urges lighting only what is needed.

choices.text: streetlights are able to illuminate a roadway after dark
choices.rationale: Both accept streetlights illuminate roads.

choices.text: some animals are active during the nighttime hours
choices.rationale: Both accept some animals are nocturnal.

explanation: The disagreement is whether cities should provide abundant night light.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Brighter streetlights, the author argues, help people feel safe, let drivers see clearly, and keep public spaces usable after dark. Light, the text says, is a basic service a city owes its residents.
stimulus.text_b: Flooding the night with light, this author warns, wastes energy, disrupts the sleep of people and the movement of nocturnal animals, and erases the stars. Cities, the text suggests, could light only what truly needs it.

prompt: How does Text 2 respond to the central claim of Text 1?

choices.text: it denies that bone chemistry preserves any record of past meals
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not deny bone chemistry records meals.

choices.text: it claims that ancient sites contain no remains of plants at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 cites charred plant remains as evidence.

choices.text: it agrees that bones are the single most complete source of diet data
choices.rationale: Text 2 disputes that bones alone are most complete.

choices.text: it argues that bones miss foods revealed only by other site evidence
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 says bones miss foods that tools and charred plants reveal.

explanation: Text 2 responds that bones overlook foods other site evidence can reveal.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: To understand what ancient people ate, this author argues, nothing beats studying their bones and teeth directly, since the chemistry left in them records a lifetime of meals. The body itself, the text says, keeps the most honest diary.
stimulus.text_b: Bones tell only part of the story, this author counters. Tools, cooking pits, and the charred remains of plants reveal foods that leave no trace in the skeleton. The fullest picture, the text argues, comes from the whole site, not the body alone.

prompt: Based on the texts, the authors would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: ancient people ate food of some kind during their lives
choices.rationale: Both accept ancient people ate food.

choices.text: tools and cooking pits can be found at ancient sites
choices.rationale: Both accept tools and pits exist at sites.

choices.text: chemistry can be measured in ancient bones and teeth
choices.rationale: Both accept bone chemistry can be measured.

choices.text: studying bones alone gives the best account of an ancient diet
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 favors bones as the best source; Text 2 says the whole site is needed.

explanation: The dispute is whether bones alone best reveal an ancient diet.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: To understand what ancient people ate, this author argues, nothing beats studying their bones and teeth directly, since the chemistry left in them records a lifetime of meals. The body itself, the text says, keeps the most honest diary.
stimulus.text_b: Bones tell only part of the story, this author counters. Tools, cooking pits, and the charred remains of plants reveal foods that leave no trace in the skeleton. The fullest picture, the text argues, comes from the whole site, not the body alone.
